title: A/B Testing & Experimentation
description: The science of controlled experiments: sample size calculation, multiple testing, peeking, variance reduction, switchback experiments, and metrics design for FAANG-scale systems.
details: A/B testing is the gold standard for causal inference in tech — every feature launch at FAANG goes through rigorous experimentation. This file covers the statistical foundations (sample size, power, MDE), multiple testing correction (Bonferroni, Benjamini-Hochberg), the peeking problem and sequential testing, variance reduction with CUPED, switchback experiments for marketplace/network effects, and metrics hierarchy design. Each concept is paired with FAANG interview questions that test both statistical knowledge and practical judgment.

sections.title: A/B Testing Fundamentals
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: Randomly split users into control and treatment groups, measure the difference in a metric, and use statistics to determine if the difference is real or due to chance.

Sample size formula: n = (Z_{α/2} + Z_β)² · 2σ² / δ²

Where Z_{α/2} = 1.96 (α=0.05), Z_β = 0.84 (power=80%), δ = MDE (minimum detectable effect), σ² = variance of the metric.

[Practical Workflow] Workflow: Define baseline rate → choose MDE (what effect is worth shipping?) → set α=0.05 (Type I error) → set power=1-β=0.8 (Type II error) → calculate n. If n exceeds available traffic, either increase MDE, lower power, or use variance reduction (CUPED).
sections.codeBlock:
from statsmodels.stats.power import TTestIndPower
import numpy as np

# Sample size calculation
analysis = TTestIndPower()

# Parameters
mde = 0.02         # minimum detectable effect (2% relative change)
baseline = 0.10    # baseline conversion rate (10%)
effect_size = mde / np.sqrt(baseline * (1 - baseline) * 2)

n = analysis.solve_power(
    effect_size=effect_size,
    alpha=0.05,
    power=0.80,
    alternative='two-sided'
)
print(f'Required sample per variant: {int(np.ceil(n))}')

# Manual calculation (for binary outcomes)
def sample_size_binary(p, mde, alpha=0.05, power=0.80):
    za = stats.norm.ppf(1 - alpha/2)
    zb = stats.norm.ppf(power)
    p_avg = p * (1 + mde/2)  # pooled proportion
    n = (za + zb)**2 * 2 * p_avg * (1 - p_avg) / (p * mde)**2
    return int(np.ceil(n))

n_manual = sample_size_binary(0.10, 0.02)
print(f'Manual calculation: {n_manual}')

sections.title: Multiple Testing Correction
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: If you test 20 metrics at α=0.05, you expect 1 false positive by random chance. Multiple testing corrections control this family-wise error rate.

FWER vs FDR:

  • FWER (Family-wise Error Rate): P(at least one false positive). Controlled by Bonferroni (α/m) and Holm-Bonferroni (sequential). Very conservative.
  • FDR (False Discovery Rate): E[V/R] — expected proportion of false positives among rejected hypotheses. Controlled by Benjamini-Hochberg (BH). Less conservative, more power.

[Concrete Numbers] Concrete: Testing 10 metrics, BH procedure: sort p-values (0.001, 0.004, 0.01, ...). Compare pᵢ ≤ i·0.05/10 = i·0.005. Reject p₁=0.001 (≤0.005), p₂=0.004 (≤0.01). Stop at p₃=0.01 (>0.015). FDR controlled at 5% — we expect 5% of discovered effects to be false.
sections.codeBlock:
from statsmodels.stats.multitest import multipletests
import numpy as np

# Simulate p-values for 20 tests (2 are truly significant)
np.random.seed(42)
p_values = np.concatenate([
    np.random.uniform(0, 0.1, 2),   # real effects (small p)
    np.random.uniform(0.4, 1, 18)   # null (large p)
])

# Bonferroni correction
reject_bonf, p_corrected_bonf, _, _ = multipletests(
    p_values, method='bonferroni'
)
print(f'Bonferroni: {np.sum(reject_bonf)} significant')

# Benjamini-Hochberg (FDR control)
reject_bh, p_corrected_bh, _, _ = multipletests(
    p_values, method='fdr_bh'
)
print(f'BH (FDR): {np.sum(reject_bh)} significant')

# Holm-Bonferroni (less conservative than Bonferroni)
reject_holm, p_corrected_holm, _, _ = multipletests(
    p_values, method='holm'
)
print(f'Holm-Bonferroni: {np.sum(reject_holm)} significant')

# Sidak correction (slightly less conservative than Bonferroni)
reject_sidak, p_corrected_sidak, _, _ = multipletests(
    p_values, method='sidak'
)
print(f'Sidak: {np.sum(reject_sidak)} significant')

sections.title: Peeking Problem
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: If you check the A/B test results every day and stop as soon as p < 0.05, you'll eventually always find significance — even if there's no real effect. The Type I error rate balloons to 20-30% or more.

Solutions:

  • Fixed horizon: Pre-register the sample size. Don't analyze until all data is collected. Simple but inflexible.
  • Sequential testing: Always-valid p-values based on martingale theory. You can check any time with valid inference. Used by Google's OEC framework. Most practical for FAANG.
  • Pocock / O'Brien-Fleming bounds: Spend α unevenly across interim analyses. O'Brien-Fleming: very little α spent early, most at the final analysis. More conservative in early looks, preserves overall α.

[Concrete Numbers] Concrete: 30-day A/B test checked daily. With naive peeking, Type I error jumps from 5% to ~26%. Using O'Brien-Fleming with 5 interim looks, the early significance thresholds are extremely strict (p < 0.00005 at first look), preserving 5% overall.
sections.codeBlock:
# Sequential testing simulator: shows peeking inflation
import numpy as np
from scipy import stats

def simulate_peeking(n_days=30, n_per_day=1000, effect_size=0, n_sims=1000):
    false_positives = 0
    for sim in range(n_sims):
        data_a = np.random.normal(0, 1, n_days * n_per_day)
        data_b = np.random.normal(effect_size, 1, n_days * n_per_day)
        
        # Peek every day
        significant = False
        for day in range(1, n_days + 1):
            a = data_a[:day * n_per_day]
            b = data_b[:day * n_per_day]
            _, p = stats.ttest_ind(a, b)
            if p < 0.05:
                significant = True  # would stop early
                break
        if significant:
            false_positives += 1
    
    return false_positives / n_sims

# With no effect (H₀ true), Type I error should be 5%
# With peeking, it's much higher:
type_i = simulate_peeking(effect_size=0.0)
print(f'Type I error with peeking: {type_i:.3f} (should be 0.05)')

# Fixed horizon (no peeking):
def simulate_fixed(n=30000, effect_size=0):
    a = np.random.normal(0, 1, n)
    b = np.random.normal(effect_size, 1, n)
    _, p = stats.ttest_ind(a, b)
    return p < 0.05

fixed_fp = np.mean([simulate_fixed() for _ in range(1000)])
print(f'Fixed horizon Type I: {fixed_fp:.3f}')

sections.title: Variance Reduction (CUPED)
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: Use pre-experiment data (covariates) to explain some of the variance in the outcome. This reduces the noise, making it easier to detect a signal. Can reduce required sample size by 30-50%.

Formula: Y_cv = Y - θ·(X - E[X]). Optimal θ minimizes Var(Y_cv): θ* = Cov(Y,X) / Var(X).

The covariate X must be: (1) pre-experiment (measured before treatment starts), (2) correlated with the outcome Y, and (3) unaffected by the treatment. Common covariates: pre-experiment metric value, user historical behavior, demographic features.

[Concrete Numbers] Concrete: A/B test on revenue per user. Pre-experiment revenue (X) has Corr(Y,X) = 0.6. θ* = 0.6·σ_Y/σ_X. Var(Y_cv) = (1-0.6²)·Var(Y) = 0.64·Var(Y). Sample size reduction = 1 - 0.64 = 36%. The same experiment needs 36% fewer users with CUPED.
sections.codeBlock:
import numpy as np
from scipy import stats

# CUPED implementation
np.random.seed(42)
n = 10000

# Pre-experiment covariate (e.g., pre-period metric)
X = np.random.normal(100, 15, n)

# Outcome = baseline + treatment effect + noise
treatment = np.concatenate([np.zeros(n//2), np.ones(n//2)])
Y = 100 + 2*treatment + 0.6*X + np.random.normal(0, 10, n)

y_control = Y[treatment == 0]
y_treatment = Y[treatment == 1]
x_control = X[treatment == 0]
x_treatment = X[treatment == 1]

# Without CUPED (standard t-test)
t_stat, p_standard = stats.ttest_ind(y_control, y_treatment)
print(f'Standard: p = {p_standard:.4f}')

# CUPED adjustment
# Compute optimal θ on control group
cov_yx = np.cov(y_control, x_control)[0, 1]
var_x = np.var(x_control)
theta = cov_yx / var_x
print(f'Optimal θ = {theta:.3f}')

# Adjust both groups
Y_adj_control = y_control - theta * (x_control - np.mean(x_control))
Y_adj_treatment = y_treatment - theta * (x_treatment - np.mean(x_treatment))

t_stat, p_cuped = stats.ttest_ind(Y_adj_control, Y_adj_treatment)
print(f'CUPED: p = {p_cuped:.4f}')
print(f'Variance reduction: {1 - np.var(Y_adj_control)/np.var(y_control):.1%}')

sections.title: Switchback Experiments
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: In a marketplace (Uber, Doordash), randomizing individual users doesn't work because of network effects — a rider's experience depends on driver supply, which is affected by the treatment. Solution: randomize by time interval instead.

Design considerations:

  • Switch interval: Too short → carryover effects (treatment bleeds into next interval). Too long → few switch events (low statistical power). 30-60 minutes is common.
  • Washout period: A brief window (10-15 min) between switches where no experiment runs, allowing the system to reset.
  • Analysis: Account for autocorrelation in time series. Use robust standard errors (Newey-West) or mixed effects models with random time effects. Don't naively treat each data point as independent — they're correlated within each switch block.

[Concrete Numbers] Concrete: Uber testing a surge pricing change. Randomizing by 30-min intervals across 2 weeks. 30-min washout between intervals. Analysis using clustered standard errors by time interval. Achieves 80% power with 2 weeks of data.
sections.codeBlock:
# Switchback analysis — clustered standard errors
import statsmodels.api as sm
import numpy as np

# Simulated switchback data
# time_interval: which 30-min block (1..T)
# treatment: 0/1 alternating per interval
# outcome: metric of interest

np.random.seed(42)
T = 100  # 100 time intervals

# Alternating treatment assignment
treatment = np.tile([0, 1], T // 2 + 1)[:T]
time_interval = np.arange(T)

# Outcome with treatment effect + time autocorrelation
error = np.random.normal(0, 1, T)
for t in range(1, T):
    error[t] += 0.3 * error[t-1]  # AR(1) autocorrelation

y = 10 + 0.5 * treatment + error

# WRONG: naive t-test (assumes independence)
t_stat, p_naive = sm.stats.ttest_ind(
    y[treatment == 1], y[treatment == 0]
)
print(f'Naive test (ignores autocorr): p = {p_naive:.4f}')

# CORRECT: OLS with clustered standard errors
X = sm.add_constant(treatment)
model = sm.OLS(y, X).fit(cov_type='cluster', cov_kwds={'groups': time_interval})
print(f'Clustered SE: p = {model.pvalues[1]:.4f}')

# Simulate washout by dropping first 2 obs after each switch
# (not shown: more sophisticated Bayesian structural time series)

sections.title: Metrics & Guardrails
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: Not all metrics are created equal. Every experiment needs exactly one primary metric (the north star), a few secondary metrics (what might also change), and guardrail metrics (must not regress).

Metrics hierarchy:

  • Primary metric: The single metric that determines ship/no-ship. Usually the business north star (e.g., revenue per user, 7-day retention). Must be measurable, sensitive, and robust.
  • Secondary metrics: Other metrics that might be affected. Used for: (1) understanding why the primary metric moved, (2) discovering unintended consequences, (3) generating hypotheses for future experiments.
  • Guardrail metrics: Metrics that must not significantly regress. Examples: latency (P99), crash rate, support tickets, unregisters. If a guardrail is violated, the experiment doesn't ship — even if the primary metric moves positively.

A/A tests: Run control vs control to verify the experimentation system. The metric should be significant exactly α% of the time. If you see significance > α%, your system has bias (e.g., sample ratio mismatch, time-of-day effects, bot traffic). Always run A/A tests after changing the experimentation platform.
sections.codeBlock:
# A/A test: verify the experimentation system
import numpy as np
from scipy import stats

# Run control vs control — should find significance ~5% of the time (α=0.05)
n_aa_tests = 1000
p_values = []

for _ in range(n_aa_tests):
    # Two groups from same distribution (no effect)
    group_a = np.random.normal(100, 10, 10000)
    group_b = np.random.normal(100, 10, 10000)
    _, p = stats.ttest_ind(group_a, group_b)
    p_values.append(p)

false_positive_rate = np.mean(np.array(p_values) < 0.05)
print(f'A/A false positive rate: {false_positive_rate:.3f} (expected 0.05)')

# Metrics hierarchy example: decision framework
def should_ship(primary_p, effect_size, guardrails):
    # guardrails: dict of metric -> {'p': p_val, 'regression': bool}
    
    if primary_p > 0.05:
        return False, "Primary metric not significant"
    
    for name, g in guardrails.items():
        if g['p'] < 0.05 and g['regression']:
            return False, f"Guardrail {name} regressed"
    
    return True, f"Ship it! Effect = {effect_size:+.2%}"

sections.title: FAANG Interview Q&A
sections.description:
[Interview Angle] Q1: "Your A/B test shows p=0.04 but you checked every day for 30 days — is the result valid?" — Netflix L5

No, it's likely not valid. This is the peeking problem. If you check the p-value every day and stop when p < 0.05, the effective Type I error rate balloons to 20-30% over 30 days. The p=0.04 result could easily be a false positive — you stopped when the noise happened to cross the threshold. To fix this: use sequential testing with always-valid p-values (e.g., via the mSPRT — mixture Sequential Probability Ratio Test). Or pre-register a fixed sample size and don't peek. Or use O'Brien-Fleming spending boundaries that allocate most of the α budget to later analyses. The safest practical approach for production: use a sequential testing framework (Google's OEC, Netflix's implementation) that allows continuous monitoring with valid inference.

[Interview Angle] Q2: "How do you calculate sample size for an A/B test?" — Meta E4

The formula: n = (Z_{α/2} + Z_β)² · 2σ² / δ². For a binary metric (e.g., conversion rate), σ² = p(1-p) where p is the baseline rate. For α=0.05, Z=1.96; for 80% power, Z=0.84. If baseline conversion is 10% and we want to detect a 2% relative lift (δ=0.002, σ²=0.09), n ≈ 35K per variant. Key considerations: (1) Matters what you assume — if the actual variance is higher than estimated, the test will be underpowered. Use historical data to estimate variance. (2) Account for multiple variants — Bonferroni correction increases n. (3) Account for network effects — switchback or cluster-randomized designs need larger n due to intra-cluster correlation. (4) Don't forget the MDE — it should be the minimum effect that makes the feature worth shipping, not what you expect to see.

[Interview Angle] Q3: "What is CUPED and how does it reduce sample size?" — Netflix L5 (they love CUPED)

CUPED (Controlled Experiment Using Pre-Experiment Data) reduces the variance of the outcome metric by controlling for pre-experiment covariates. The adjusted metric is Y_cv = Y - θ·(X - E[X]), where X is a pre-experiment covariate correlated with Y. The optimal θ* = Cov(Y,X)/Var(X) minimizes Var(Y_cv). The variance reduction factor is 1 - Corr(Y,X)² — if the correlation between pre and post metric is 0.6, variance is reduced by 36%, meaning the sample size is also reduced by 36%. Common covariates: the same metric measured in the pre-period (highest correlation), user segment indicators, or week-over-week changes. CUPED requires: (1) X measured before the experiment starts, (2) X unaffected by treatment (guaranteed by timing), and (3) stable relationship between X and Y over time. The biggest implementation gotcha: compute θ on the control group only, then apply to both groups — computing θ on pooled data can introduce bias if the treatment affects the covariances.

[Interview Angle] Q4: "How would you run an A/B test on a two-sided marketplace?" — Uber L5

Two-sided marketplaces (Uber, Doordash, Airbnb) have interference — treating one user affects other users. If Uber raises prices for rider A, that rider might take fewer trips, reducing earnings for nearby drivers, who then become less available for rider B (even if B is in the control group). The treatment "bleeds" into the control. Three solutions: (1) Switchback experiments: Randomize by time interval (30-min blocks). Treat all users in a block as A or B. This eliminates interference within each block but introduces time-series autocorrelation. (2) Market-level randomization: Randomize entire cities or regions. Fewer randomization units → less statistical power. (3) Cluster-based randomization: Partition the market into geographically isolated clusters (e.g., neighborhoods) and randomize at the cluster level. Use cluster-robust standard errors for analysis. For spillover effects, use the exposure Markov decision process (EMDP) framework or design-based interference models. The industry trend is switchback + market-level randomization as a complement.

[Interview Angle] Q5: "You're testing 20 metrics. How do you handle multiple testing?" — Google L4

I handle multiple testing based on the role of each metric. The primary metric gets one shot at α=0.05 — no correction needed since it's pre-specified and the decision is based on it. For the 20 metrics, I group them: (1) Primary: no correction, (2) Secondary (exploratory): use Benjamini-Hochberg FDR control at q=0.1 — this controls the expected proportion of false discoveries without being as conservative as Bonferroni, (3) Guardrails: use Bonferroni correction (α/m) because each guardrail failure independently blocks shipping — a false positive on a guardrail might lead to shipping a harmful feature if we incorrectly think the guardrail is fine. In practice at Google scale, dashboards frequently show 100+ metrics. We color-code: green = significant after BH correction, yellow = raw p < 0.05 but not significant after correction, gray = not significant. The decision to ship is based on the primary metric; all other metrics inform interpretation.

sections.title: References
sections.description:
• Trustworthy Online Controlled Experiments (KDD 2013) — Kohavi, Longbotham, Sommerfield, Henne: the definitive A/B testing paper
  • CUPED paper — Deng, Xu, Kohavi, Walker (2013): "Improving the Sensitivity of Online Controlled Experiments"
  • Sequential testing — Johari et al. (2017): "Always Valid Inference"
  • Switchback experiments — Bojinov et al. (2021): design and analysis of switchback experiments
  • Online Controlled Experiments at Scale — Kohavi, Tang, Xu (2020): book-length treatment
